class_schedule.topic: What Is Educational Psychology? Science of Learning and Teaching
learning_objectives: Define educational psychology and its role in improving education
Describe the scientific method as applied to educational research
Identify the major areas of educational psychology: learning, development, motivation, assessment

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational psychology is the scientific study of how people learn and how to teach effectively. It provides the evidence base that should inform every instructional decision, every technology design, and every educational policy.
key_concepts: Educational psychology as a discipline: history and scope
The science of learning: evidence-based practice vs. edu-myths
Major areas: cognitive development, learning theory, motivation, assessment, classroom management
Hattie's meta-analyses: what actually works in education
Common learning myths: learning styles, 10% brain use, digital natives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Myth-busting activity: evaluate five common educational beliefs against research evidence and identify which are supported and which are myths
setup_requirements: List of educational myths and facts

review.value: Educational decisions should be guided by the best available research evidence, not tradition, intuition, or marketing claims. Educational psychology provides the evidence base.

Hattie's synthesis of 1,800+ meta-analyses ranks influences on achievement. High-impact strategies (feedback, metacognition, peer tutoring) are often low-cost. Low-impact strategies (class size reduction, technology alone) are often expensive.

class_schedule.topic: Cognitive Development: Piaget, Vygotsky, and Beyond
learning_objectives: Describe Piaget's stages of cognitive development and their educational implications
Explain Vygotsky's zone of proximal development and scaffolding
Apply developmental theory to the design of age-appropriate instruction
readings: Vygotsky, L.S. - Mind in Society Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding cognitive development is essential for designing instruction that meets learners where they are. A lesson appropriate for a 10-year-old may frustrate a 6-year-old or bore a 16-year-old.
key_concepts: Piaget's stages: sensorimotor, preoperational, concrete operational, formal operational
Constructivism: learners actively build knowledge through interaction with the environment
Vygotsky's ZPD: the gap between independent ability and guided ability
Scaffolding: temporary support that is gradually removed as competence grows
Neo-Piagetian and information processing perspectives on development
lecture_notes.demonstration: ZPD mapping exercise: for a specific learning objective, identify what a 10-year-old can do alone, what they can do with help, and what they cannot yet do. Design scaffolding for the ZPD.

review.value: Instruction should target the ZPD: challenging enough to require effort but not so challenging that it produces frustration even with support. This is where learning happens most efficiently.

Effective scaffolding is temporary and responsive: provide just enough support for the learner to succeed, then gradually remove it as competence builds. The goal is independence, not dependence on the scaffold.

class_schedule.topic: Memory, Cognition, and Effective Study Strategies
learning_objectives: Describe the multi-store model of memory and its implications for instruction
Explain evidence-based study strategies: retrieval practice, spaced practice, interleaving
Debunk common ineffective study strategies: rereading, highlighting, cramming
readings: Dunlosky, J. et al. - Improving Students' Learning with Effective Learning Techniques

lecture_notes.introduction: Decades of cognitive psychology research have identified study strategies that dramatically improve learning and strategies that feel effective but are not. Educational psychologists have a responsibility to teach students HOW to learn, not just what to learn.
key_concepts: Sensory memory, working memory, long-term memory
Encoding strategies: elaboration, organization, dual coding
Retrieval practice: testing as a learning event (testing effect)
Spaced practice: distributed study over time (spacing effect)
Interleaving: mixing practice of different topics
Ineffective strategies: rereading, highlighting, massed practice (cramming)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Memory experiment: demonstrate the spacing effect by having half the class mass-practice a set of terms and half use spaced practice, then test retention after 20 minutes

review.value: Taking a test on material produces stronger long-term retention than restudying the same material for the equivalent amount of time. Testing is a learning strategy, not just an assessment strategy.

Strategies that feel harder during learning (spacing, interleaving, retrieval practice) produce better long-term retention than strategies that feel easy (rereading, highlighting). The feeling of fluency during rereading is deceptive.

learning_objectives: Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic motivation
Apply self-determination theory to educational design
Evaluate the impact of rewards and grades on motivation
readings: Deci, E. & Ryan, R. - Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivation is what makes people start learning; engagement is what keeps them going. Understanding motivation theories allows educators and designers to create conditions that promote sustained effort and genuine interest.
key_concepts: Intrinsic motivation: engaging in an activity for its inherent satisfaction
Extrinsic motivation: engaging for external rewards or to avoid punishment
Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness
Growth mindset vs. fixed mindset (Dweck)
The overjustification effect: how rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation
Attribution theory: how students explain their success and failure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Motivational redesign: take three demotivating educational practices (points-for-compliance, public failure, forced competition) and redesign each to support autonomy, competence, and relatedness

review.value: Autonomy: give students meaningful choices. Competence: provide optimal challenge with clear feedback. Relatedness: create a sense of belonging and connection. When all three are met, intrinsic motivation flourishes.

Students who believe ability is fixed ('I'm not a math person') avoid challenge and give up easily. Students who believe ability grows through effort persist through difficulty and learn more. Language matters: praise effort and strategy, not intelligence.

learning_objectives: Describe the components of social-emotional learning (CASEL framework)
Explain how classroom climate affects learning outcomes
Design learning environments that support social-emotional development
readings: CASEL - What Is SEL? Framework overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Learning is not purely cognitive. Emotions, social relationships, and psychological safety profoundly affect whether and how deeply students learn. Social-emotional learning is not separate from academic learning — it enables it.
key_concepts: CASEL five competencies: self-awareness, self-management, social awareness, relationship skills, responsible decision-making
Psychological safety: the belief that one can take risks without punishment
Belonging and its impact on academic achievement
Trauma-informed practices: understanding how adverse experiences affect learning
Teacher-student relationships as the foundation of effective education
lecture_notes.demonstration: Climate assessment: evaluate a classroom scenario for psychological safety indicators and design three specific changes to improve the learning climate

review.value: Students who fear being wrong will not take intellectual risks. Classrooms where errors are treated as learning opportunities, not failures, produce deeper engagement and learning.

A meta-analysis by Walton & Cohen showed that a brief belonging intervention for minority students in college improved GPA by 0.3 points and halved the achievement gap. Belonging is not a soft skill — it is a learning prerequisite.

class_schedule.topic: Transfer of Learning: From Classroom to Real World
learning_objectives: Explain the conditions that promote transfer of learning
Distinguish between near transfer and far transfer
Design instruction that maximizes the likelihood of transfer
readings: Perkins, D. & Salomon, G. - Teaching for Transfer

lecture_notes.introduction: The ultimate goal of education is not performance on tests but the ability to apply learning in new, real-world contexts. Transfer is the holy grail of education — and it is notoriously difficult to achieve.
key_concepts: Near transfer: applying learning to similar situations
Far transfer: applying learning to very different situations
Conditions for transfer: deep understanding, multiple contexts, explicit abstraction
Hugging and bridging: strategies for promoting near and far transfer
Why transfer often fails: inert knowledge, context-dependent learning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Transfer design exercise: take a learning objective and design instruction that maximizes transfer through multiple examples, varied contexts, explicit principle extraction, and real-world application

review.value: Whitehead's concept: knowledge that is possessed but never used. Students may 'know' the quadratic formula but cannot apply it outside a math class. Instruction must explicitly teach when and how to apply knowledge.

Bridging strategies help learners see connections: ask 'Where else could you use this?', present problems in diverse contexts, require students to generate their own examples, and explicitly teach the abstract principle underlying specific cases.

questions.question: A student studies for an exam by rereading the textbook chapter three times. The student feels confident because the material seems 'familiar.' Using cognitive psychology research, explain why this strategy is likely to produce poor exam performance and recommend a more effective alternative.
questions.answer: Rereading creates a feeling of fluency — the material seems familiar, leading the student to overestimate their understanding. But recognition ('this looks familiar') is not the same as retrieval ('I can recall and apply this'). Dunlosky et al.'s research rates rereading as a low-utility strategy because it produces shallow encoding without deep processing. More effective alternative: retrieval practice — close the book and try to recall the key concepts from memory, then check accuracy. This activates the testing effect: the act of retrieving information strengthens the memory trace and reveals gaps in understanding. Even better: combine retrieval practice with spaced practice by spreading study sessions over several days.

rubric.key: Explains why rereading creates false confidence (fluency illusion)
Identifies retrieval practice as a superior alternative with explanation
References research evidence (testing effect, spacing effect)

questions.question: A 6-year-old student cannot understand that the amount of water stays the same when poured from a short, wide glass into a tall, thin glass. Using Piaget's theory, explain this observation and its implications for teaching science to young children.
questions.answer: The child is in Piaget's preoperational stage (approximately ages 2-7) and has not yet developed conservation — the understanding that quantity remains the same despite changes in appearance. The child focuses on a single dimension (height of the water) and cannot mentally reverse the action (centration and irreversibility). Implications for teaching science to young children: (1) Use concrete, hands-on materials rather than abstract explanations. (2) Provide multiple representations: let the child pour the water back and forth to experience the reversibility. (3) Do not expect children to reason abstractly about conservation until the concrete operational stage (around age 7-11). (4) Focus instruction on exploration and observation rather than abstract concepts that require conservation and reversibility.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies preoperational stage and conservation
Provides practical teaching implications appropriate to the stage

questions.question: A teacher implements a class-wide reward system where the student with the highest test score each week gets a prize. Using self-determination theory, analyze the potential negative consequences of this system and propose an alternative that supports intrinsic motivation.
questions.answer: SDT analysis: this system undermines all three basic needs. Autonomy: students have no choice — the single competition pathway is imposed. Competence: only one student can 'win,' so most students experience failure every week, undermining their sense of competence. Students who consistently lose may stop trying (learned helplessness). Relatedness: competition sets students against each other, damaging peer relationships and classroom community. Additionally, the overjustification effect may reduce intrinsic interest in learning by making grades about winning prizes rather than understanding. Alternative: replace individual competition with personal growth goals. Each student sets a target improvement from their previous score. Students who meet their personal goal earn recognition. This supports competence (achievable goals), autonomy (personal goal-setting), and relatedness (everyone can succeed without others failing).

rubric.key: Applies SDT to identify specific harms to autonomy, competence, and/or relatedness
Identifies overjustification effect or other motivational damage
Proposes a specific alternative that supports intrinsic motivation

questions.question: Define 'inert knowledge' and explain why it is a common problem in education. Describe one instructional strategy that combats inert knowledge by promoting transfer.
questions.answer: Inert knowledge is knowledge that is possessed but cannot be accessed or applied when needed in real-world situations. It is common because traditional instruction often teaches concepts in a single context (the textbook, the classroom) without connecting them to multiple applications. Students learn 'for the test' and then cannot use the knowledge in different settings. Strategy: varied practice across multiple contexts — present the same concept in 3-5 different real-world scenarios rather than practicing with identical textbook problems. For example, teach statistical concepts using sports data, medical data, and business data rather than only abstract number sets. This builds multiple retrieval pathways and helps students recognize when the concept applies, combating the context-dependence that produces inert knowledge.

rubric.key: Describes a specific transfer-promoting strategy with example

major_assignments.title: Evidence-Based Teaching Strategy Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a teaching or learning strategy (e.g., retrieval practice, peer tutoring, metacognitive strategies, cooperative learning, formative feedback). Write a research synthesis evaluating the evidence base for the strategy. Include: theoretical foundations, meta-analytic evidence of effectiveness, conditions under which the strategy works best and worst, and practical implementation guidelines for educators. Conclude with a critical evaluation of limitations and research gaps.
objectives: Synthesize research evidence on a specific educational psychology topic
Evaluate the quality and applicability of research findings
Translate research into practical guidelines for educators
Write a professional research synthesis with proper academic conventions
deliverables: Research synthesis paper (3,000 words in APA format)

criteria: Clear explanation of the underlying psychological theory
Historical development of the strategy traced
Meta-analytic evidence accurately reported and interpreted
Effect sizes and practical significance discussed
Boundary conditions and moderating variables identified
Guidelines grounded in the evidence reviewed
Limitations of existing research honestly assessed
Scholarly writing quality with proper APA formatting
Thorough research synthesis with strong evidence base
Clear connection between theory and practice

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on educational psychology concepts and applications

participation.description: In-class activities, experiments, and discussion contributions

midterm_examination.description: Covers development, cognition, memory, and study strategies

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam emphasizing motivation, SEL, transfer, and application to educational technology

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. In-class experiments and activities cannot be made up. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Extensions require 48-hour advance notice.
course_policies.collaboration: Study groups are encouraged. All written work must be individually authored with proper citation.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism or submitting another's work results in a zero and referral to the academic integrity office. Cite all sources.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access for research. No specialized software required.
